detailed_analysis: markdown_yaml_metadata_block.py is a small, cohesive shared-primitives module (283 lines, under the 300-line cap) that consolidates the Markdown-frontmatter parse/render helpers previously hand-rolled across nine adapters. It contains one trivial exception subclass (AdapterParseError) and fourteen module-level functions, every one of which is well under the 40-line limit and carries full type annotations on its public surface. The module is well-named per the project's two-word convention, has an explicit __all__, and documents its public API in the docstring. There are no hardcoded secrets, no injection vectors, no global mutable state, and no bare-except swallowing. The single CRITICAL-class concern is absent. The notable structural observations are: (1) a mild DRY smell — the three-step 'normalize_markdown_text -> FRONTMATTER_RE.match -> yaml_load' pattern is repeated across split_frontmatter (lines 153-178), frontmatter_for_render (lines 239-247), and extract_pair_id_from_md (lines 256-263), each with a slightly different post-match policy; and (2) the lazy imports of parser_bounds inside make_yaml and split_frontmatter, documented as a deliberate cycle-break (parser_bounds imports AdapterParseError from this module). The lazy import is the correct defect-avoidance choice given the dependency direction, not a smell, but it is worth recording. Security is clean: this module is a lossless YAML/Markdown codec layer; the secret_policy (NFR-15) is correctly NOT this module's responsibility — it is enforced in mcp_secret_policy.py and adoption/privacy_gate.py at the egress boundaries. The module is not listed in architecture.md §4 module map or §9 traceability table, which is a documentation-currency gap given it is now a shared dependency of parser_bounds and the Markdown adapters.

cyclic_dependencies: markdown_yaml_metadata_block <-> parser_bounds: parser_bounds imports AdapterParseError at module load (parser_bounds.py:38); this module imports parser_bounds lazily inside make_yaml (line 70) and split_frontmatter (line 151) precisely to break the cycle. No module-load-time cycle exists. Documented in docstrings.

findings.evidence:
split_frontmatter (153-171): text = normalize_markdown_text(text); match = FRONTMATTER_RE.match(head); ... loaded = yaml_load(raw_frontmatter)
framractter_for_render (239-247): prior_text = normalize_markdown_text(prior_text); prior_match = FRONTMATTER_RE.match(prior_text); ... loaded = yaml_load(raw)
extract_pair_id_from_md (256-262): text = normalize_markdown_text(text); match = FRONTMATTER_RE.match(text); loaded = yaml_load(match.group(1))
findings.problem: The normalize -> FRONTMATTER_RE.match -> yaml_load sequence is repeated in three functions, each diverging only in its post-match policy (raise vs empty-dict vs None, and whether the frontmatter window bound is applied). The module's whole purpose is to deduplicate this exact prelude across adapters; ironically the prelude is duplicated three times internally. Note also that frontmatter_for_render (240) and extract_pair_id_from_md (257) call FRONTMATTER_RE.match on the full text WITHOUT enforce_frontmatter_window, whereas split_frontmatter (159) applies the window bound — an inconsistency in DoS protection across the three entry points, not just a style nit.

normalize_markdown_text adds no behaviour over strip_bom_prefix; it exists solely so call sites read 'normalize' at parse time. Both are exported in __all__, doubling the public surface for one operation. The docstring justifies it on readability grounds, which is defensible, but it is a second name for one concept (mild tension with the project's 'one concept, one word' naming rule). Low severity; record only.

The notion of 'value means absent' is hardcoded inline as three disjuncts (None, empty string, empty list). It is a three-operator boolean (within the W1 threshold of >3, so not flagged as W1) but encodes a domain rule — 'what counts as an empty metadata field' — as an anonymous expression rather than a named predicate. A value of 0, False, or {} would NOT be treated as absent, which is a deliberate but undocumented asymmetry. Minor maintainability/clarity note, not a defect.

deviations.description: markdown_yaml_metadata_block.py does not appear in the §4 module map nor the §9 traceability table, yet it is now a Layer-3 shared adapter primitive imported by 9+ *_io adapters and is the source of AdapterParseError consumed by parser_bounds. §9 states 'Any future requirement that has no carrying module is a defect in either the requirement or the architecture' and §1 promises the document is kept true. The module (and parser_bounds.py) are undocumented in the architecture, a currency gap. Not a code defect in the scoped file; recorded against the governance artifact's stated invariant.

split_frontmatter raises AdapterParseError with the message '{label} frontmatter must be a YAML mapping' (lines 174-177). The structured-error invariant I-4 / NFR-13 requires every reported failure to name the artifact_id, the tool, and the cause. This message carries the adapter 'label' (tool) and cause but no customization_artifact_id. This is acceptable IF the calling adapter enriches the exception with pair_id context before logging; this module is below the layer that knows pair_id, so the partial context is defensible. Flagged as a boundary observation, not a confirmed violation — verification requires the adapter call sites (out of scope).

verdict_summary: Clean, cohesive shared primitive module; no CRITICAL/MAJOR/WARNING findings, only a DRY note, a thin-alias smell, and an inline-emptiness smell. code_score 0.75 capped by maintainability. Architecture docs (§4/§9) are stale: the module is undocumented despite being a shared Layer-3 dependency.
